title: Reviewing What Claude Wrote
subtitle: Trust but verify — always check before committing

sections.content:
Claude Code is powerful, but it's not perfect. Just like you'd review a colleague's code before merging it, you should **always review what Claude wrote** before committing it to your project.

This isn't about distrust — it's about being a responsible developer. Even experienced human programmers have their code reviewed. The habit of reviewing changes is what separates someone who uses AI well from someone who uses it recklessly.

The good news: you already know the tool for this. It's `git diff`.

sections.instruction: After Claude makes changes to your project, review them with git diff. This shows you exactly what was added, removed, or modified — line by line.

sections.content: Reading a diff is a skill. Here's a quick refresher on what you're looking at:
keyPoints: Green lines (starting with +) are code that was added
Red lines (starting with -) are code that was removed
Test in the browser — does the change actually work as expected?
If you're satisfied, stage and commit the changes
If something's off, tell Claude what to change — iterate!

sections.question: In a git diff, what color represents added lines?
sections.explanation: In a git diff, added lines are shown in green (with a + prefix) and removed lines are shown in red (with a - prefix). This makes it easy to see what changed at a glance.

sections.prompt: Before committing Claude's changes, always run `git _____` to review them.

sections.instruction: Use this code review checklist every time Claude makes changes. Make it a habit — it only takes a minute and saves hours of debugging.

items.text: I've run git diff to see what changed
items.hint: Green = added, red = removed. Scan for anything unexpected.

items.text: I understand what the changes do
items.hint: If not, ask Claude: 'Explain what you just changed and why'
Does it look right? Does it work as expected? Try edge cases.

items.text: I'm ready to commit (or iterate further)
items.hint: If something's wrong, tell Claude. If it's good, commit it.

completionMessage: Review before you commit. This one habit will save you from countless bugs and unexpected changes. You're now practicing the same code review discipline that professional teams use every day.
